verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B encodes the beta subunit of mitochondrial ATP synthase (F1Fo complex), canonically located in the mitochondrial inner membrane facing the matrix. However, a well-documented ectopic surface expression phenomenon ('ecto-ATP synthase') places catalytically active ATP synthase complexes, including the beta subunit, on the plasma membrane outer leaflet of various cell types including endothelial cells, hepatocytes, adipocytes, and tumor cells. This has been confirmed by surface biotinylation and flow cytometry on intact non-permeabilized cells, and the surface complex is targeted therapeutically (e.g., angiostatin binds ecto-ATP synthase beta subunit on endothelial cell surfaces). Checking contextual buckets: cell_state_induced — partially applies in tumor/activated endothelium; tissue_restricted_surface — not strictly single lineage; lysosomal_exocytosis — not the mechanism; dual_localization — best fits, dominant mitochondrial pool with documented PM pool; stable_surface_attachment — not secreted. The mechanism of PM trafficking is unconventional (no signal peptide or TM domain of its own), but surface presence is wash-resistant and functionally validated.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Mechanism of ecto-ATP synthase PM trafficking is unresolved; surface display may be cell-type or activation-state restricted rather than universal.
